questions.text: What is the purpose of the pragma directive in Solidity?

options.text: It imports external libraries like OpenZeppelin
It specifies which compiler version the code is compatible with, preventing compilation with incompatible versions
It declares the license under which the code is distributed
It defines the contract's name and its constructor

questions.explanation: The pragma directive (e.g., pragma solidity ^0.8.20) tells the compiler which version of Solidity the code is written for. It ensures the contract is not compiled with an incompatible compiler that could produce incorrect bytecode.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The pragma directive (e.g., pragma solidity ^0.8.20) tells the compiler which version of Solidity the code is written for. It ensures the contract is not compiled with an incompatible compiler that could produce incorrect bytecode.

questions.text: What is a state variable in Solidity?

options.text: A variable that is only used inside a single function and discarded after execution
A variable declared at the contract level whose value is permanently stored on the blockchain
A variable that stores the current state of the Ethereum network
A variable that can only be read, never written to

questions.explanation: State variables are declared at the contract level and stored in the contract's persistent storage on the blockchain. They persist between transactions and cost gas to modify.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

State variables are declared at the contract level and stored in the contract's persistent storage on the blockchain. They persist between transactions and cost gas to modify.

questions.text: Which function visibility allows the function to be called from OUTSIDE the contract but NOT from inside the same contract?

options.text: public
external
internal
private

questions.explanation: external functions can only be called from outside the contract (by users, dApps, or other contracts). They cannot be called from within the contract using this.functionName() - only external calls. This slightly reduces gas costs compared to public functions.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

external functions can only be called from outside the contract (by users, dApps, or other contracts). They cannot be called from within the contract using this.functionName() - only external calls. This slightly reduces gas costs compared to public functions.

questions.text: What is the difference between a view function and a pure function?

options.text: view costs gas; pure is free
view can read state variables; pure cannot read or modify any state
pure can modify state; view cannot
There is no difference; they are synonyms

questions.explanation: A view function can read blockchain state (state variables, balances, block info) but cannot modify it. A pure function cannot read OR write state - it operates only on its input parameters. Both are free to call externally.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

A view function can read blockchain state (state variables, balances, block info) but cannot modify it. A pure function cannot read OR write state - it operates only on its input parameters. Both are free to call externally.

questions.text: In the HelloWorld contract, why does owner use the immutable keyword?

options.text: It makes the variable impossible to read by anyone except the owner
It tells the compiler the variable is set only in the constructor, allowing it to be stored in bytecode instead of storage - saving gas on every read
It allows the owner to be changed later by a special admin function
It prevents the variable from being included in the contract's ABI

questions.explanation: immutable variables can only be assigned in the constructor and are stored directly in the deployed bytecode. Reading an immutable variable costs far less gas than reading a regular storage variable.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

immutable variables can only be assigned in the constructor and are stored directly in the deployed bytecode. Reading an immutable variable costs far less gas than reading a regular storage variable.

questions.text: What happens if you try to call updateGreeting from an address that is NOT the owner?

options.text: The greeting is updated anyway - the require statement is just a warning
The transaction reverts - the state is NOT changed, but gas is still consumed for the attempted computation
The transaction succeeds but emits a different event
The contract self-destructs to protect its data

questions.explanation: When require fails, the entire transaction reverts - all state changes are undone. However, the gas used up to the point of failure is NOT refunded. The caller pays for the attempted computation, and the contract state remains exactly as it was.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

When require fails, the entire transaction reverts - all state changes are undone. However, the gas used up to the point of failure is NOT refunded. The caller pays for the attempted computation, and the contract state remains exactly as it was.

questions.text: "In Solidity, a variable declared as uint256 that is never assigned a value will contain garbage data from the blockchain's previous state, just like uninitialized variables in C or C++."
questions.explanation: 1 mark for correctly stating FALSE and explaining that Solidity variables have default zero values guaranteed by the EVM.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark for correctly stating FALSE and explaining that Solidity variables have default zero values guaranteed by the EVM.

questions.text: "If a function is marked as view, it can still modify state variables as long as it doesn't use the public visibility modifier."
questions.explanation: 1 mark for correctly stating FALSE and explaining that view is a compile-time constraint preventing state modification, unrelated to visibility.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark for correctly stating FALSE and explaining that view is a compile-time constraint preventing state modification, unrelated to visibility.

questions.text: The following contract is deployed. The deployer (address 0xAAA) provides "Hello" as the constructor argument. Then a user (address 0xBBB) calls the function mystery("World"). What is the EXACT output of the event emitted? Write the full event log with all parameters.
questions.explanation:
The event emitted is: Changed(indexed who = 0xBBB, from = "Hello", to = "World")
Explanation: The constructor set stored to "Hello". When 0xBBB calls mystery("World"), old captures the current value of stored ("Hello"), stored is updated to "World", and the event is emitted with msg.sender = 0xBBB, old = "Hello", and input = "World".
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Model Answer

The event emitted is: Changed(indexed who = 0xBBB, from = "Hello", to = "World")

Explanation: The constructor set stored to "Hello". When 0xBBB calls mystery("World"), old captures the current value of stored ("Hello"), stored is updated to "World", and the event is emitted with msg.sender = 0xBBB, old = "Hello", and input = "World".

Grading: 1 mark for correct values (who=0xBBB, from="Hello", to="World"). 1 mark for correctly identifying the event name (Changed) and parameters.
